preamble: The debuccalization of the voiceless labiodental fricative /f/ to the glottal fricative /h/ is a classic example of lenition. This shift involves moving the site of friction from the lips and teeth to the glottis. It is famously documented in the transition from Latin to Spanish and also appears in the historical development of Japanese and other language families.

examples.note: Latin initial f- became [h] in Old Spanish, which later became silent in the modern language.
Initial f- weakening: Latin 'to do' → Spanish 'to do'.
Historical *p underwent lenition to [ɸ] and then [h] in modern Japanese (except before /u/).

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Penny, R. (2002). A History of the Spanish Language.
Vance, T. J. (2008). The Sounds of Japanese.
